question: Your college is organizing a large annual fest. You've been asked to volunteer. Which role excites you the most?

A.text: Managing the event app, digital registrations, and tech setup

B.text: Designing banners, stage decor, and the social media vibe

C.text: Handling budgets, sponsorships, and vendor negotiations

D.text: Coordinating logistics, volunteers, and event operations

question: A startup founder visits your campus and offers internship projects. Which project would you pick?

A.text: Build a tool that automates student timetable scheduling

B.text: Create a brand identity and marketing campaign for their product

C.text: Analyze their revenue model and suggest a growth strategy

D.text: Set up their supply chain process and operations workflow

question: Your team is stuck on a group project because nobody understands the data. What do you do?

A.text: Break down the data, find patterns, and present clear insights to the team

B.text: Suggest a visual or creative way to represent the data that everyone can understand

C.text: Take charge, assign parts to each member, and set a timeline

D.text: Start compiling whatever data exists and begin building the report immediately

question: You are given a free weekend to work on anything you like for your department club. What would you do?

A.text: Research an unsolved problem in your field and prepare a report

B.text: Design a new concept or idea and present it as a poster or prototype

C.text: Plan and pitch a new initiative to the club president

D.text: Organize the club's existing resources, files, and upcoming tasks

question: A classmate's business idea has potential but their presentation is very unorganized. You decide to help. What's your first step?

A.text: Review their numbers and assumptions to see if the idea is actually viable

B.text: Redesign the slides to make them visually compelling and easy to follow

C.text: Rally a few classmates to form a team and divide responsibilities

D.text: Build a checklist of all missing parts and start filling them in one by one

question: You notice that attendance at your college club events has been dropping. What's your approach?

A.text: Gather feedback, map attendance trends, and identify why people stopped coming

B.text: Brainstorm a fresh, exciting event concept that feels totally different from the usual

C.text: Convene a team meeting, assign tasks, and personally follow up to ensure everyone acts

D.text: Immediately start reaching out to students, booking venues, and scheduling the next event

question: Your professor asks the class to solve a real community problem as a semester project. What role do you naturally take?

A.text: Researcher who digs into the root cause with data and analysis

B.text: Ideator who comes up with the most original and impactful solution

C.text: Project lead who keeps the team aligned and on track

D.text: Doer who starts building, testing, and delivering results early

question: Your team wins a competition and gets funding to build something. The first thing you focus on is:

A.text: Running a feasibility analysis and studying similar projects that worked or failed

B.text: Sketching out what the product or experience will look and feel like

C.text: Defining roles, responsibilities, and milestones for the team

D.text: Getting a basic working version or prototype out as quickly as possible

question: A new student joins your project group midway. How do you handle it?

A.text: Assess their strengths and figure out where they add the most value to the work

B.text: Welcome them warmly and brainstorm fresh ideas together to energize the group

C.text: Brief them on roles, assign them a task, and ensure they are integrated smoothly

D.text: Hand them a clear task list so they can start contributing right away

question: Two teammates in your group are having a conflict over how to approach the project. You:

A.text: Listen to both sides carefully and suggest the option with the strongest logical basis

B.text: Propose a creative middle ground that blends the best of both ideas

C.text: Mediate the discussion, make a decision, and keep the team moving forward

D.text: Ask both to move on and focus on completing the work within the deadline

question: You are assigned to represent your department at an inter-college quiz. How do you prepare?

A.text: Study past question papers, find patterns, and prepare topic-wise summaries

B.text: Create engaging mind maps and visual study tools to make learning fun

C.text: Form a study group, assign topics, and practice as a team

D.text: Set a daily study target and grind through the material consistently until quiz day

question: Your department is launching a campaign to reduce plastic use on campus. What's your contribution?

A.text: Study the data on plastic waste on campus and identify the top problem areas

B.text: Design eye-catching posters and videos to spread awareness creatively

C.text: Coordinate volunteers, set targets, and drive the campaign from the front

D.text: Set up collection drives, track progress daily, and ensure targets are met

question: You are asked to write an article for the college newsletter about a recent campus event. You approach it by:

A.text: Reviewing feedback and data from attendees to write a factual, insight-driven piece

B.text: Writing a vivid, story-driven narrative that captures the spirit and emotion of the event

C.text: Interviewing key organizers and crafting a piece that highlights team leadership

D.text: Quickly drafting the article, getting it reviewed, and submitting it ahead of deadline

question: You have one month before placement season. You decide to spend it:

A.text: Identifying which companies suit your profile and analysing what skills they look for

B.text: Building a unique portfolio or side project that makes you stand out

C.text: Mentoring peers while also preparing yourself and running group mock sessions

D.text: Setting daily targets, practising aptitude tests, and completing your resume step by step

question: Your college hackathon allows teams of four. After forming your team, what's the first thing you do?

A.text: Map out the problem space, research existing solutions, and identify the biggest gap

B.text: Brainstorm wild, outside-the-box ideas before narrowing down to the best one

C.text: Define each person's strengths, assign roles, and set check-in milestones

D.text: Start building a rough version immediately and iterate from there

question: Your exams are in two weeks. A classmate who scored well last semester offers to help. What kind of help do you prefer?

A.text: Give me a crisp cheat sheet of the most important points — I'll do the rest

B.text: Explain each concept in depth so I truly understand the logic behind it

C.text: Walk me through solved problems and real examples step by step

D.text: Let me teach it back to you so I can test my own understanding

question: You come across a new topic in your syllabus you know nothing about. How do you tackle it?

A.text: Watch a 5-minute summary video and move on — I'll come back if needed

B.text: Read the full chapter, take detailed notes, and understand every section

C.text: Find a practical exercise or case study related to the topic and learn by doing

D.text: Search for a visual diagram or framework that summarizes the whole thing quickly

question: A workshop on public speaking is being offered. You sign up. What do you hope to get out of it?

A.text: A list of the top five tips I can apply immediately in my next presentation

B.text: Deep knowledge of how communication works — body language, tone, structure, psychology

C.text: Live practice sessions where I can speak and get real-time feedback

D.text: Video recordings of expert speakers I can analyse and learn from at my own pace

question: You have just one hour before a class test on a topic you haven't studied. What do you do?

A.text: Skim the key headings, definitions, and formulas — done in 30 minutes

B.text: Read through the chapter carefully and absorb as much as possible

C.text: Attempt previous year questions immediately and learn from the mistakes

D.text: Ask a friend to quiz me verbally and explain anything I get wrong

question: You are learning to use a new software tool for your college project. What's your go-to approach?

A.text: Check a quick-start guide or two-minute tutorial and begin using it right away

B.text: Go through the full official documentation to understand every feature

C.text: Open the tool and start experimenting — figure it out by trial and error

D.text: Follow along with a hands-on project tutorial that shows real use cases

question: Your professor finishes a lecture but you're still confused about one concept. What do you do next?

A.text: Google a quick explanation or summary — usually one article is enough to clarify it

B.text: Read multiple explanations, textbook chapters, and research the concept thoroughly

C.text: Try applying the concept to a real-world example until it clicks

D.text: Discuss it with a classmate and explain it to each other until it makes sense

question: A senior shares a 300-page reference book for an upcoming project. Your reaction is:

A.text: Skip to the summary sections and key highlights — that's all I need

B.text: Read it cover to cover — I want to understand the full picture before I start

C.text: Use it as a reference while working on the project rather than reading it upfront

D.text: Search for a summarized version or a recorded lecture on the same topic

question: You are preparing for a group presentation. How do you get your part ready?

A.text: Read a few sources, jot down key points, and prepare concise slides quickly

B.text: Research deeply, cross-reference multiple sources, and build a solid narrative

C.text: Rehearse it out loud multiple times and present it to a friend for feedback

D.text: Use diagrams, examples, and scenarios to make the content feel more real

question: Your college offers two elective courses. One is a 5-hour intensive crash course, the other is a 3-month deep-dive. You choose:

A.text: The crash course — I learn fast and I just need the essentials

B.text: The 3-month course — I want to master the subject properly

C.text: Whichever one includes live projects, field visits, or real work

D.text: I would check the syllabus and pick based on what the job market expects

question: You want to learn photography as a new skill. How do you start?

A.text: Watch a 10-minute beginner video and start clicking photos right away

B.text: Study the theory of lighting, composition, and camera settings before touching the camera

C.text: Just pick up the camera and learn by experimenting with different shots

D.text: Join a photography walk or workshop where you practice outdoors with guidance

question: You are asked to revise an entire subject for a major exam in five days. You plan to:

A.text: Create a tight schedule covering only the most important topics and formulas

B.text: Revisit every chapter and make sure I understand everything from scratch

C.text: Solve lots of past papers and practice questions to prepare through doing

D.text: Form a study group and teach each other difficult topics to reinforce learning

question: A TED Talk you watch introduces a fascinating idea you hadn't considered before. You:

A.text: Remember the key takeaway and move on — it was an interesting idea

B.text: Go down a research rabbit hole — read articles, books, and debates on it

C.text: Think about how this idea applies to something happening in your own life or field

D.text: Share it with a friend and discuss what it means for both of you

question: You receive feedback that your assignment lacked depth. Your response is:

A.text: Look up what specific points were missing and fix just those sections

B.text: Rewrite the whole thing with much more research and richer explanations

C.text: Look for a strong example of this type of work and model your revision after it

D.text: Ask the professor exactly what 'depth' means so you don't miss the mark again

question: You want to learn basic accounting for your startup idea. Which approach suits you best?

A.text: Read a one-page explainer on profit, loss, and balance sheets and start tracking expenses

B.text: Take a full online course and understand all the principles before applying anything

C.text: Start recording your startup's actual transactions and learn what each entry means

D.text: Use a free accounting tool and figure out how it works as I enter real data

question: A friend describes a book that changed the way they think about money. You:

A.text: Ask for the top three lessons from the book — I don't need to read the whole thing

B.text: Order the book and plan to read it cover to cover with annotations

C.text: Ask how they applied the lessons in real life and try to do the same

D.text: Search for a podcast or interview with the author for a condensed version

question: You are handed a project with no clear instructions, just a vague goal. What's your reaction?

A.text: I love it — I'll invent my own approach and make something unexpected

B.text: I'll first build a clear plan, define steps, and create a framework before starting

C.text: I'll dig into the available data and research to figure out the best path forward

D.text: I'll ask clarifying questions, then start executing once I have enough direction

question: Your team needs to decide between two equally priced solutions. How do you approach the decision?

A.text: Go with the one that feels more innovative and interesting to work on

B.text: Compare both using a structured checklist of criteria before deciding

C.text: Analyse the pros and cons of each with data, case studies, and evidence

D.text: Let the person with the most relevant experience make the final call

question: It's a slow work week with no urgent tasks. What do you naturally find yourself doing?

A.text: Tinkering on a side idea or making something just for the fun of it

B.text: Organising pending tasks, updating documents, and planning the next sprint

C.text: Reviewing past project outcomes and identifying what could have been done better

D.text: Learning something new that might be useful in an upcoming project

question: A friend asks for career advice. They have three job offers and can't decide. You help by:

A.text: Encouraging them to follow the role that excites them most creatively

B.text: Building a decision matrix with salary, growth, culture, and location scores

C.text: Researching each company's trajectory, reviews, and market positioning

D.text: Asking them what matters most to them personally and guiding from there

question: You are asked to plan a surprise celebration for a departing colleague. You immediately:

A.text: Brainstorm a theme and a unique personal touch that will make it memorable

B.text: Make a checklist: venue, food, invitations, time — one task at a time

C.text: Check what events have worked well in the past and model this after that

D.text: Ask a few colleagues what they would enjoy and plan based on the responses

question: Your work environment is being redesigned. What kind of workspace would you thrive in?

A.text: An open, flexible space with whiteboards, colour, and space to move around

B.text: A quiet, organised desk with clear systems and minimal distractions

C.text: A research-friendly space with dual monitors, access to data, and documentation

D.text: A collaborative zone where I can discuss, debate, and co-create ideas

question: Your manager tells you that your last project exceeded expectations. What did you most likely do?

A.text: Added something original that nobody had thought of before

B.text: Followed the process rigorously and delivered everything on time without gaps

C.text: Dug deeper than required and discovered insights that changed the approach

D.text: Stayed aligned with the goal and made sure every stakeholder was happy

question: During a group discussion, the team is stuck and going in circles. What do you do?

A.text: Throw out a completely different angle that shifts the whole conversation

B.text: Suggest breaking the discussion into smaller defined sub-questions with a clear agenda

C.text: Ask the group to step back and look at what the actual data or evidence says

D.text: Summarise where everyone agrees and help find common ground from there

question: You are asked to describe your ideal job in one sentence. Which resonates most?

A.text: A role where I can invent, design, and express original ideas every day

B.text: A role with clear systems, defined goals, and measurable outcomes

C.text: A role where I can dig into complex problems and produce well-researched insights

D.text: A role where I can learn new things constantly and adapt to new challenges

question: Your phone's storage is almost full. How do you handle it?

A.text: Delete whatever looks old and move on — I'll deal with the rest later

B.text: Sort through everything folder by folder and follow a system to clean it up

C.text: Check what's taking the most space, analyse the breakdown, and delete strategically

D.text: Back it all up to the cloud and then clear everything in one go

question: You're assigned to write a report that will be presented to senior leadership. You begin by:

A.text: Thinking about how to make it visually compelling and memorable to read

B.text: Creating an outline with sections, headings, and a clear writing plan

C.text: Gathering all relevant data, reviewing past reports, and building arguments from evidence

D.text: Starting with the key message I want to land and working backwards from there

question: A project you are working on hits a major unexpected problem. Your first instinct is to:

A.text: Reframe the problem as an opportunity and brainstorm creative workarounds

B.text: Map out the impact of the problem and work through a step-by-step recovery plan

C.text: Trace back to find the root cause before doing anything else

D.text: Immediately inform the team so everyone is aligned and can respond together

question: You have two tasks: one routine and repetitive, one exploratory and uncertain. Where do you lean?

A.text: Strongly toward the exploratory one — the uncertainty is exciting

B.text: Toward the routine one — I like knowing exactly what to do and doing it well

C.text: Toward the exploratory one — I love figuring things out through analysis

D.text: I will finish the routine task first and then explore with a fresh mind

question: You just finished a long project. How do you wind down and reflect?

A.text: Celebrate with the team and immediately start thinking about the next creative idea

B.text: Write a brief retrospective with lessons learned and file it for future reference

C.text: Review the results against the original goals and analyse what worked and what didn't

D.text: Take a break and let the insights settle naturally before jumping into anything new

question: You overhear two colleagues debating a strategic decision. You instinctively want to:

A.text: Jump in and suggest a completely new angle they haven't considered yet

B.text: Bring structure to the debate by listing the key factors they should weigh

C.text: Listen carefully and wait to see if there is evidence or data that can resolve the debate

D.text: Summarise both perspectives to help them see where they actually agree
